name: Fudo Myoo
description: Fudo Myoo, also known as Acala in Sanskrit, is a wrathful deity prominent in Shingon Buddhism and other esoteric traditions in Japan. He is revered as one of the Wisdom Kings (Myoo), fierce protectors of the Buddhist Dharma. Fudo Myoo is often depicted as a dark-skinned figure, wreathed in flames, holding a sword in his right hand and a rope in his left. These implements symbolize his power to cut through ignorance and bind evil forces, respectively. His fierce appearance is not meant to inspire fear, but rather to demonstrate his unwavering resolve to protect sentient beings and guide them towards enlightenment. He stands on a rock, representing his immovable nature and commitment to his vows. Fudo Myoo is invoked for protection against negative influences, overcoming obstacles, and achieving spiritual transformation. He embodies the transformative power of anger and the ability to conquer inner demons.
subtitle: The Immovable Wisdom King

keyMyths.title: Fudo Myoo and the Demons
keyMyths.description: One popular myth tells of Fudo Myoo's battles against various demons and evil spirits that threaten to disrupt the peace and harmony of the world. He wields his sword to vanquish these malevolent entities, his flames consuming their negativity. His rope binds those who refuse to repent, giving them a chance to reflect on their actions and turn towards the path of righteousness. This myth highlights his role as a protector and enforcer of justice.
keyMyths.source: Saunders, E. Dale. *Mudra: A Study of Symbolic Gestures in Japanese Buddhist Sculpture*. Princeton University Press, 1985.

keyMyths.title: Fudo Myoo's Vow
keyMyths.description: It is said that Fudo Myoo made a vow to remain in the realm of suffering until all beings are liberated from their karmic burdens. He willingly embraces the darkness and negativity of the world to guide others towards enlightenment. This selfless dedication and unwavering commitment to the welfare of all beings is a central aspect of his character and a testament to his profound compassion, even in his wrathful form. This vow underscores his role as a bodhisattva in esoteric Buddhism.
keyMyths.source: Payne, Richard K., ed. *Re-envisioning Buddhism*. Oxford University Press, 2014.

keyMyths.title: The Kurikara Sword
keyMyths.description: The Kurikara sword, often depicted as a sword entwined with a dragon, is a key symbol associated with Fudo Myoo. This sword represents the power to cut through ignorance and delusion, the root causes of suffering. The dragon symbolizes wisdom and strength. By wielding this sword, Fudo Myoo severs the bonds of attachment and helps individuals to achieve clarity and understanding. The myth of the Kurikara sword emphasizes the importance of discernment and the ability to overcome mental obstacles.
keyMyths.source: Getty Research Institute. *Grove Art Online*. Oxford University Press, Accessed 2023.

sources.author: Saunders, E. Dale
sources.work: Mudra: A Study of Symbolic Gestures in Japanese Buddhist Sculpture
